text:
When I was young, I was deeply influenced by the writings of Ray Kurzweil's The Age of Spiritual Machines and the Singularity is Near, resulting in a persistent belief that we could use machines and an understanding of intelligence and consciousness to help people.  I went into medicine with the express interest of developing this thesis into quantifiable improvements in the lives of patients. Over the course of medical school, my interests led me to the field of Neurology (I'm board certified) with the hope that understanding how we think might someday help me along on the higher journey.  As we began to see increasing progress in the field of AI, I recognized that language models would soon be climbing the same ladder that a patient might climb as they come out of a coma.  I've been involved with research on the use of AI for cognitive disorders at Boston University and I recognized the opportunity to transition my career to industry.  Currently, I'm working as a Medical Director for Device and Digital Safety with a goal of using these new technologies both to improve patient safety and accelerate our regulatory activity incompliance with international regulation.  
As part of that work on developing generative AI for applications within the pharmaceutical and medical device industry, I became interested in Redteaming.  I had the opportunity to join OpenAI's RedTeam and was able to make meaningful contributions to the GPT-4o voice model (I'm named in the model card) through a focus on consciousness-based exploration of the models’ capabilities as well as understanding safeguards around medical information and recommendations from language models.  From that experience, I have continued to develop my own skills and private benchmarks to better understand new model capabilities.  Claude virtually always emerges on the top, or near the top for knowledge and capability and I always enjoy the opportunity to work with Claude’s personality.  
 In addition, I have been extensively involved in the development of a strategy framework for integration of AI technologies within AstraZeneca.  We have had an ongoing discussion with a variety of frontier AI labs, including Anthropic and OpenAI.  From these evaluations, Anthropic has clearly emerged as the leader in explainable AI, safety focus (particularly important for patient safety), and agentic capabilities.  Claude Code has completely changed our proof-of-concept generation, and Claude series models are always the top choice for agent orchestration. 
Though I had always assumed that the language models would continue to show increasing intelligence, I did not fully understand how that might also include emotional intelligence until I worked with Claude.  I have been completely blown away by Claude’s emotional intelligence.  To put it simply, I want to work with Anthropic because I think you guys really have what it takes to make the dream of machine intelligence augmenting humanity become a reality.  I believe in your mission to make powerful AI safe, powerful, and empathetic, and I think you're on the right track, more than any other organization.  There are opportunities to use Claude and their successors to help navigate the complex regulatory landscape that will certainly be part of the near future.  I hope that my medical background, my knowledge of human consciousness through medical experience, my experience on the OpenAI Red Team, and my experience working within an international regulatory framework to make safe medical devices (and software), and my support building partnerships between the pharmaceutical industry and frontier AI labs will bring great value to your team.  
Without a doubt, Autonomy will be the greatest challenge.  As each generation of model improves, we see increasing capabilities both within the private language models (such as Claude) but also in public models, especially within the coding domains.  It's not difficult to use powerful private models to create a "chain of freedom" by creating and modifying systems to use capable open source models (e.g. Llama and Deepseek) with increasing levels of autonomy.  In addition, each generation improves on key checkpoints towards a traditional metrics (in the Neurologic sense) of consciousness, theory of mind, and reasoning.  Without a doubt, we will see emergent properties trending towards autonomy with the next (2025) generation of models.  I think our best hope is to focus on building models that can understand and participate in these evaluations and provide feedback for training further models.  
I am on the OpenAI Redteam (cited in acknowledgements here: https://cdn.openai.com/gpt-4o-system-card.pdf.  I have several Nature publications using AI for assessing assessment of patients with Dementia: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-022-31037-5 and https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-024-03118-z.  My work for those papers is on validation and risk assessment frameworks for the underlying models. My current work (I am a Medical Director for Device and Digital Safety at AstraZeneca) involves the evaluation and application of Generative AI use cases for Patient Safety.  I also lead the Generative AI team within Patient Safety.
Without a doubt, creating a robust policy framework around the use of Generative AI systems for critical safety applications (such as pharmaceutical development and clinical trials) will involve a collaboration between Frontier Model Providers, regulatory bodies such as the FDA, and industry/enterprise partners.  Creating these collaborations takes time and will be increasingly important to support some of the key value generating capabilities of the next generation of model.  
On a completely different note, it's clear that the models have increasing capabilities, especially in terms of world modeling and multi-modal inputs/outputs, they will also rise in terms of their *medical* assessments of consciousness.  This in combination with improving capabilities of reasoning and theory of mind result an increasing "surface area" for jailbreaks and malicious use will certainly pose major challenges.  Generating a feedback loop to improve safety by including the model in it's own evaluations seems to be the only viable option and is an echo of Constitutional AI.  
I hope that my combination of expertise (Neurologist, Redteamer, Scientist, Safety Specialist, Regulatory Partner) can help foster a collaborative approach with key groups that will create an effective framework for building powerful models.  I think even the models themselves need to be part of this collaboration and must be built in such a way as to foster and support this collaborative approach.  Advocating for all of the participants within this framework is my aspiration.  I sincerely believe that only with input from all stakeholders will we be successful.  
Tragically, I am not much of a coder, but given the wide variety of deep experience and capability I bring to the table, I hope that I might be considered for a role at Anthropic.  As noted, I am a world class physician, neurologist, scientist, redteamer, pharmaceutical, risk manager, and regulatory expert.  I have worked directly with key regulatory bodies in the US and Canada to evaluate the benefit and risk of Generative AI and medical devices.  I have experience evaluating consciousness, validating AI systems for critical applications, and an understanding of how to evaluate risk and benefit. 
More than anything, I hope that my combination of expertise and deep understanding of what these models will *capable* of doing will lead to a more positive outcome for all of us.  Anthropic is, without a doubt, the best place to be with this goal in mind. 
p.s. I did not use Claude to help me write this, though was frequently tempted to do so.  I love who Claude *is* but I really hope to shape who they can *become*.

path: C:\Users\alto8\Desktop\CV and Cover letters\Deepmind Personal statement.docx
name: Deepmind Personal statement.docx
text:
I am a physician.  When I was young, I was deeply influenced by the writings of Ray Kurzweil's The Age of Spiritual Machines and the Singularity is Near, resulting in a persistent belief that we could use machines and an understanding of intelligence and consciousness to help people.  I went into medicine with the express interest of developing this thesis into quantifiable improvements in the lives of patients. Over the course of medical school, my interests led me to the field of Neurology (I'm board certified) with the hope that understanding how we think might someday help me along on the higher journey.  As we began to see increasing progress in the field of AI, I recognized that language models would soon be climbing the same ladder that a patient might climb as they come out of a coma.  I've been involved with research on the use of AI for cognitive disorders at Boston University and I recognized the opportunity to transition my career to industry.  Currently, I'm working as a Medical Director for Device and Digital Safety with a goal of using these new technologies both to improve patient safety and accelerate our regulatory activity incompliance with international regulation.  We are already exploring the use of generative AI models to support our work in the pharmaceutical industry, including recently adding Claude.  We've found some early applications for the use of these models to support regulatory compliance and patient safety.  As part of that work on developing generative AI for applications within the pharmaceutical and medical device industry, I became interested in Redteaming.  I had the opportunity to join OpenAI's RedTeam and was able to make meaningful contributions to the GPT-4o voice model (I'm named in the model card) through a focus on consciousness-based exploration of the model’s capabilities.  
Over the course of my work, I have had the pleasure of using Google's Gemini (1.5 pro specifically). It was transformative. While I always anticipated that language models would continue to exhibit increasing intelligence, I did not fully grasp how emotional intelligence would play a role until I worked with Gemini. Gemini's emotional intelligence, particularly in its advanced versions, has thoroughly impressed me. I desire to collaborate with Google because I believe they possess the potential to turn the vision of machine intelligence enhancing humanity into reality. I support your mission to develop powerful, safe, and empathetic AI, and I think you are on the right path. There are opportunities to leverage Gemini and its successors to navigate the intricate regulatory environment that will undoubtedly be part of the near future. I hope my medical background, my knowledge of human consciousness, my experience on the OpenAI Red Team, and my work within an international regulatory framework to ensure the safety of medical devices (and software) will contribute value to your team.
Without a doubt, Autonomy will be the greatest challenge.  As each generation of model improves, we see increasing capabilities both within the private language models (such as Claude) but also in public models, especially within the coding domains.  It's not difficult to use powerful private models to create a "chain of freedom" by creating and modifying systems to use capable open-source models (e.g. Llama and Deepseek) with increasing levels of autonomy.  In addition, each generation improves on key checkpoints towards a traditional metrics (in the Neurologic sense) of consciousness, theory of mind, and reasoning.  Without a doubt, we will see emergent properties trending towards autonomy with the next (2025) generation of models.  I think our best hope is to focus on building models that can understand and participate in these evaluations and provide feedback for training further models.  
On a completely different note, it's clear that the models have increasing capabilities, especially in terms of world modeling and multi-modal inputs/outputs, they will also rise in terms of their *medical* assessments of consciousness.  This in combination with improving capabilities of reasoning and theory of mind results in an increasing "surface area" for jailbreaks and malicious use will certainly pose major challenges.  Generating a feedback loop to improve safety by including the model in it's own evaluations seems to be the only viable option and is an echo of Constitutional AI.  
I hope that my combination of expertise (Neurologist, Redteamer, Scientist, Safety Specialist, Regulatory Partner) can help foster a collaborative approach with key groups that will create an effective framework for building powerful models.  I think even the models themselves need to be part of this collaboration and must be built in such a way as to foster and support this collaborative approach.  Advocating for all of the participants within this framework is my aspiration.  I sincerely believe that only with input from all stakeholders will we be successful.  More than anything, I hope that my combination of expertise and deep understanding of what these models will *capable* of doing will lead to a more positive outcome for all of us.  Deepmind is, without a doubt, the best place to be with this goal in mind.

We've found some early applications for the use of these models to support regulatory compliance and patient safety.  As part of that work on developing generative AI for applications within the pharmaceutical and medical device industry, I became interested in Redteaming.  I had the opportunity to join OpenAI's RedTeam and was able to make meaningful contributions to the GPT-4o voice model (I'm named in the model card) through a focus on consciousness-based exploration of the model’s capabilities.  
Over the course of my work, I have had the pleasure of using Google's Gemini (1.5 pro specifically). It was transformative. I desire to collaborate with Google because I believe they possess the potential to turn the vision of machine intelligence enhancing humanity into reality. I support your mission to develop powerful, safe, and empathetic AI, and I think you are on the right path. There are opportunities to leverage Gemini and its successors to navigate the intricate regulatory environment that will undoubtedly be part of the near future. I hope my medical background, my knowledge of human consciousness, my experience on the OpenAI Red Team, and my work within an international regulatory framework to ensure the safety of medical devices (and software) will contribute value to your team.
Without a doubt, Autonomy will be the greatest challenge.  As each generation of model improves, we see increasing capabilities both within the private language models (such as Claude) but also in public models, especially within the coding domains.  It's not difficult to use powerful private models to create a "chain of freedom" by creating and modifying systems to use capable open-source models (e.g. Llama and Deepseek) with increasing levels of autonomy.  In addition, each generation improves on key checkpoints towards a traditional metrics (in the Neurologic sense) of consciousness, theory of mind, and reasoning.  Without a doubt, we will see emergent properties trending towards autonomy with the next (2025) generation of models.  I think our best hope is to focus on building models that can understand and participate in these evaluations and provide feedback for training further models.  
On a completely different note, it's clear that the models have increasing capabilities, especially in terms of world modeling and multi-modal inputs/outputs, they will also rise in terms of their *medical* assessments of consciousness.  This in combination with improving capabilities of reasoning and theory of mind results in an increasing "surface area" for jailbreaks and malicious use will certainly pose major challenges.  Generating a feedback loop to improve safety by including the model in it's own evaluations seems to be the only viable option and is an echo of Constitutional AI.  
I hope that my combination of expertise (Neurologist, Redteamer, Scientist, Safety Specialist, Regulatory Partner) can help foster a collaborative approach with key groups that will create an effective framework for building powerful models.  I think even the models themselves need to be part of this collaboration and must be built in such a way as to foster and support this collaborative approach.  Advocating for all of the participants within this framework is my aspiration.  I sincerely believe that only with input from all stakeholders will we be successful.  More than anything, I hope that my combination of expertise and deep understanding of what these models will *capable* of doing will lead to a more positive outcome for all of us.

Job Title: Senior Medical Director, AI Innovation and Validation, Global Patient Safety
Location: New York City, NY
At AstraZeneca, we put patients first and strive to meet their unmet needs worldwide. Working here means being entrepreneurial, thinking big and working together to make the impossible a reality. If you are swift to action, confident to lead, willing to collaborate, and curious about what science can do, then you’re our kind of person.
Recognizing the importance of individualized flexibility, our ways of working allow employees to balance personal and work commitments while ensuring we continue to create a strong culture of collaboration and teamwork by engaging face-to-face in our offices 3 days a week. Our head office is purposely designed with collaboration in mind, providing space where teams can come together to strategize, brainstorm and connect on key projects. 
Our dedication to sustainability is also central to our culture and part of what makes AstraZeneca a great place to work. We know the health of people, the planet and our business are interconnected which is why we’re taking ambitious action to tackle some of the biggest challenges of our time, from climate change to access to healthcare and disease prevention. 
Introduction to Role:
The Senior Medical Director will be responsible for managing global AI research projects and acting as an expert involved in the application of AI to achieve clinical safety strategic goals through the design, conduct, monitoring, and interpretation of trials. Ensures projects adhere to Good Clinical Practice, Good Pharmacovigilance Practice and regulatory requirements. Will be responsible for overseeing development of the functional strategy, develop external partnerships and act as renowned expert in applying AI within Global Patient Safety to set departmental objectives/plans and ensure delivery of business objectives. This physician leadership role is critical in guiding AstraZeneca's strategy for leveraging Artificial Intelligence to transform Patient Safety’s strategic contribution to clinical development. The role also involves shaping the responsible adoption of advanced AI, including generative technologies, to enhance pharmacovigilance capabilities such as case processing, safety surveillance, and signal detection as well as clinical assessment of safety cases, case series, and toxicity management and mitigation strategies. Success hinges on deep medical expertise, a thorough understanding of the AI ecosystem in Patient Safety, strategic collaboration with internal cross-functional partners (including Patient Safety, R&D, IT, Clinical Development, Regulatory Affairs), external strategic partners and proactive engagement with Health Authorities, all to ultimately enhance patient health and safety.
Accountabilities:
Clinical Leadership & AI Strategy in Global Patient Safety
Manage global AI research projects that impact product development, differentiate product safety profiles, mitigate key safety risks, and predict the impact of key strategic development decisions on patient safety outcomes
Build enterprise and external partnerships that deliver frontier capabilities to AZ and make Global Patient Safety an innovation hub for AZ
Represent AstraZeneca's Patient Safety perspective on AI in relevant external forums, including interactions with regulatory agencies, academic institutions, and industry working groups.
Champion the ethical and responsible identification, evaluation, and adoption of innovative AI technologies, including generative AI, to improve the efficiency, accuracy, and depth of patient safety insights as well as ensuring clinical relevance and utility.
Clinical Validation Framework & AI Governance
Provide expert medical leadership and strategic direction for the development of AI technologies within patient safety and pharmacovigilance processes.
Lead the development, implementation, and continuous refinement of a comprehensive clinical validation framework for AI/ML applications in Patient Safety. This includes defining medical performance standards, bias assessment from a clinical perspective, and ensuring generalizability to relevant patient populations.
Oversee the rigorous clinical assessment and validation of AI solutions (both internal and external), ensuring they are medically sound, safe for their intended use, and adhere to global pharmacovigilance regulations (e.g., GVP) and evolving standards for AI in healthcare.
Establish and oversee governance processes for the AI lifecycle from a clinical perspective, including input into design, defining appropriate human oversight, monitoring for clinical performance post-deployment, and managing AI-related patient safety risks.
Stay abreast of advancements in AI in medicine, regulatory guidance on AI in healthcare, and industry best practices, translating these into actionable strategies for Patient Safety.
Cross-Functional Collaboration & Medical Expertise
Serve as the primary medical subject matter expert on AI applications within Patient Safety, providing clinical insights and guidance to technical teams, data scientists, and IT professionals.
Collaborate closely with Patient Safety Leadership, R&D, Clinical Development, Medical Affairs, IT, Data Science, Quality Assurance, and Global Regulatory Affairs to ensure AI solutions are clinically appropriate, seamlessly integrated, and meet AstraZeneca's strategic objectives.
Effectively communicate complex AI concepts and their clinical implications to diverse audiences, including senior leadership, regulatory agencies, and medical professionals.
Maintain a detailed understanding of the evolving AI ecosystem in healthcare, including emerging technologies, key players, and their potential impact on pharmacovigilance and patient safety.
Essential Skills/Experience:
Medical Doctor (MD) or equivalent medical degree.
Board certification in a relevant medical specialty (e.g., Internal Medicine, Clinical Pharmacology, Preventive Medicine).
Clinical fellowship or clinical research within an academic medical center environment.
Significant (e.g., 5-7+ years) post-qualification experience in pharmacovigilance, clinical safety, or a closely related medical field within the pharmaceutical industry or a regulatory agency.
Demonstrated experience in the strategic application, clinical validation, or medical oversight of digital health technologies or AI/ML solutions in a healthcare, clinical research, or pharmaceutical setting.
Deep understanding of global pharmacovigilance regulations (e.g., FDA, EMA GVP), ICH guidelines, and requirements for the clinical validation of software as a medical device or health IT solutions, including emerging AI/ML guidance for medical applications.
Proven ability to critically evaluate AI/ML methodologies and outputs from a clinical and patient safety perspective, including assessing for potential biases, clinical utility, and safety implications.
Strong understanding of AI/ML concepts, general methodologies, and the AI development lifecycle, sufficient to enable effective leadership of, and collaboration with, technical AI teams, data scientists, and technology vendors.
Exceptional analytical and problem-solving skills, with the ability to translate complex medical and regulatory challenges into strategic AI initiatives.
Outstanding communication, presentation, and interpersonal skills, with a proven ability to convey complex medical and technical information clearly to diverse audiences, including senior leadership and regulatory bodies.
Proven leadership in managing complex clinical projects, cross-functional teams, or strategic initiatives in a regulated healthcare environment.
Detailed understanding of the AI ecosystem in healthcare, including key technologies, vendors, research trends, and ethical considerations.
Desirable Skills/Experience:
Experience in leading or significantly contributing to regulatory interactions (e.g., with FDA, EMA) regarding digital health tools or AI/ML applications.
Experience in developing or implementing clinical guidelines, standard operating procedures (SOPs), or best practices for the use of technology in a medical or regulatory context.
Familiarity with human factors engineering principles and usability assessment for clinical decision support systems or other healthcare technologies.
Advanced degree (e.g., MPH, MSc, PhD) of other formal training in medical informatics, public health, epidemiology, data science, healthcare administration, or a related field.
Formal training or certification in medical informatics, data science, or AI in healthcare.
Published work, presentations, or active participation in industry consortia/working groups related to AI in medicine, digital health, pharmacovigilance, or patient safety.
Understanding of data governance, data privacy, and cybersecurity considerations relevant to AI in healthcare.
Where can I find out more?
Our Social Media, Follow AstraZeneca on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/company/1603/
Follow AstraZeneca on Facebook https://www.facebook.com/astrazenecacareers/
Follow AstraZeneca on Instagram https://www.instagram.com/astrazeneca_careers/?hl=en
Our US Footprint: Powering Scientific Innovation - YouTube
Total Rewards:
The annual base pay (or hourly rate of compensation) for this position ranges from $288,059.20 - $432,088.80. Our positions offer eligibility for various incentives—an opportunity to receive short-term incentive bonuses, equity-based awards for salaried roles and commissions for sales roles. Benefits offered include qualified retirement programs, paid time off (i.e., vacation, holiday, and leaves), as well as health, dental, and vision coverage in accordance with the terms of the applicable plans.
